mnemonics.title: Find Operation - Who Leads?
mnemonics.content: To FIND the representative of a set, follow parent pointers until you reach a node that points to itself (the root). This root is the set's leader.

mnemonics.title: Union - Join Forces
mnemonics.content: To UNION two sets, find each set's root, then make one root point to the other. The roots merge, combining both groups.

mnemonics.title: Path Compression - Flatten the Tree
mnemonics.content: PATH COMPRESSION: During find, make every node point directly to the root. Like flattening a hierarchy—everyone reports straight to the boss.

mnemonics.title: Union by Rank - Smaller Tree Joins Bigger
mnemonics.content: UNION BY RANK: Always attach the shorter/smaller tree under the taller root. Like merging small teams into large ones—keeps trees balanced.

mnemonics.title: Amortized O(1) - Inverse Ackermann
mnemonics.content: With both optimizations (path compression + union by rank), operations run in near-constant time: O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function—practically O(1).

acronyms.usage: The formal name for the data structure that tracks a set of elements partitioned into disjoint (non-overlapping) sets.
Alternative name emphasizing the two main operations: Union (merge sets) and Find (locate representative).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a forest where each tree represents one set. Each node points to its parent, and tree roots are the set representatives. Initially, every node is its own single-node tree.
Represent the structure as an array where parent[i] tells you the parent of node i. Roots have parent[root] = root. Drawing circles with arrows showing parent relationships helps visualize the chain.
Rank represents the estimated height of each tree. When unioning, compare ranks and attach the smaller rank tree under the larger. Visualize as balancing scale—put lighter side under heavier side.

analogies.analogy: Think of Union Find like political parties. To find which party someone belongs to (Find), you trace up to the party leader (root). When two members from different parties marry and merge parties (Union), one leader becomes subordinate to the other.
Social media uses Union Find to track friend groups. When two people connect (Union), their entire networks merge. Finding mutual friends means tracing back to the same group root.

Imagine a map of states. Union merges neighboring states into regions. Find checks if two cities are in the same region. Path compression is like creating direct flights from every city to the regional capital.
When carving paths in a maze, Union Find tracks which cells are connected. Opening a wall (Union) merges two regions. Checking if two points connect (Find) determines if a path exists.
